preamble: The phonetic transformation from the close front rounded vowel /y/ to the voiced labio-velar approximant /w/ involves a process of consonantalization, where a vowel takes on a consonantal, glide-like quality. This shift typically includes a backing of the vowel's articulation and a change in manner from a syllabic vowel to a non-syllabic approximant, while often retaining the rounding feature.
phoneticEffects: Consonantalization, Backing, Vowel to Glide, Approximantization

examples.note: Shift occurs before the vowel /u/ and also before /a/.
Proto-Guang *y became w before round vowels.

sources: Cook, E.-D. (1984). A Sarcee Grammar. Vancouver, British Columbia: University of British Columbia Press.
Snider, K. L. (1990). The consonants of proto-Guang. Journal of West African Languages, 20(1), 3-26.
